description: A HashMap is an immutable key-value data structure that provides efficient lookup, insertion, and deletion operations. It uses a Hash Array Mapped Trie (HAMT) internally for structural sharing and optimal performance.
A function that updates a value based on its current state. Takes an Option representing the current value and returns an Option representing the new value.
This type-level utility extracts the key type `K` from a `HashMap<K, V>` type.
This type-level utility extracts the value type `V` from a `HashMap<K, V>` type.
This type-level utility extracts the entry type `[K, V]` from a `HashMap<K, V>` type.
Checks if a value is a HashMap.
Creates a new empty `HashMap`.
examples:
import { HashMap } from "effect"

const map = HashMap.empty<string, number>()
console.log(HashMap.isEmpty(map)) // true
console.log(HashMap.size(map)) // 0

description: Constructs a new `HashMap` from an array of key/value pairs.
examples:
import { HashMap } from "effect"

const map = HashMap.make(["a", 1], ["b", 2], ["c", 3])
console.log(HashMap.size(map)) // 3
console.log(HashMap.get(map, "b")) // Option.some(2)

description: Creates a new `HashMap` from an iterable collection of key/value pairs.
examples:
import { HashMap } from "effect"

const entries = [["a", 1], ["b", 2], ["c", 3]] as const
const map = HashMap.fromIterable(entries)
console.log(HashMap.size(map)) // 3
console.log(HashMap.get(map, "a")) // Option.some(1)

description: Checks whether the `HashMap` contains no entries.
Safely lookup the value for the specified key in the `HashMap` using the internal hashing function.
Lookup the value for the specified key in the `HashMap` using a custom hash.

Unsafely lookup the value for the specified key in the `HashMap` using the internal hashing function. Warning: This function throws an error if the key is not found. Use `HashMap.get` for safe access that returns `Option`.
Checks if the specified key has an entry in the `HashMap`.
Checks if the specified key has an entry in the `HashMap` using a custom hash.
Checks if an element matching the given predicate exists in the given `HashMap`.
examples:
import { HashMap } from "effect"

const hm = HashMap.make([1, "a"])
HashMap.hasBy(hm, (value, key) => value === "a" && key === 1) // -> true
HashMap.hasBy(hm, (value) => value === "b") // -> false

description: Sets the specified key to the specified value using the internal hashing function.
Returns an `IterableIterator` of the keys within the `HashMap`.
Returns an `IterableIterator` of the values within the `HashMap`.
Returns an `Array` of the values within the `HashMap`.
Returns an `IterableIterator` of the entries within the `HashMap`.
Returns an `Array<[K, V]>` of the entries within the `HashMap`.
Returns the number of entries within the `HashMap`.

Creates a transient mutable `HashMap` for efficient batched updates. Apply updates to the returned map, then call `endMutation` to finish the mutation window and use the result as an immutable `HashMap`.
Marks the `HashMap` as immutable, completing the mutation cycle.

Runs a batch of updates against a transient mutable copy of the `HashMap` and returns the finalized immutable result. The callback may call mutation-oriented helpers such as `set` and `remove` on the transient map.

Sets or removes the specified key using an update function. The update function receives `Some(value)` when the key exists or `None` when it does not. Returning `Some(newValue)` stores the value, and returning `None` removes the key or leaves it absent.

Sets or removes the specified key using a precomputed hash and an update function. The update function receives `Some(value)` when the key exists or `None` when it does not. Returning `Some(newValue)` stores the value, and returning `None` removes the key or leaves it absent.
Updates the value of the specified key within the `HashMap` if it exists.
Combines two `HashMap`s into one. Entries from `that` are inserted into `self`; when both maps contain an equal key, the value from `that` replaces the value from `self`.
Remove the entry for the specified key in the `HashMap` using the internal hashing function.
Removes all entries in the `HashMap` which have the specified keys.
Sets multiple key-value pairs in the `HashMap`.
examples:
import { HashMap } from "effect"

const map1 = HashMap.make(["a", 1], ["b", 2])
const newEntries = [["c", 3], ["d", 4], ["a", 10]] as const // "a" will be overwritten
const map2 = HashMap.setMany(map1, newEntries)

console.log(HashMap.size(map2)) // 4
console.log(HashMap.get(map2, "a")) // Option.some(10)
console.log(HashMap.get(map2, "c")) // Option.some(3)

description: Maps over the entries of the `HashMap` using the specified function.
Chains over the entries of the `HashMap` using the specified function. NOTE: the hash and equal of both maps have to be the same.
Applies the specified function to the entries of the `HashMap`.
Reduces the specified state over the entries of the `HashMap`.
Filters entries out of a `HashMap` using the specified predicate.
examples:
import { HashMap } from "effect"

const map1 = HashMap.make(["a", 1], ["b", 2], ["c", 3], ["d", 4])
const map2 = HashMap.filter(map1, (value) => value % 2 === 0)

console.log(HashMap.size(map2)) // 2
console.log(HashMap.has(map2, "b")) // true
console.log(HashMap.has(map2, "d")) // true
console.log(HashMap.has(map2, "a")) // false

description: Filters out `None` values from a `HashMap` of `Options`s.
Maps over the entries of the `HashMap` using the specified filter and keeps only successful results.
Returns the first element that satisfies the specified predicate, or `None` if no such element exists.
Checks if any entry in a hashmap meets a specific condition.
examples:
import { HashMap } from "effect"

const map = HashMap.make(["a", 1], ["b", 2], ["c", 3])

console.log(HashMap.some(map, (value) => value > 2)) // true
console.log(HashMap.some(map, (value) => value > 5)) // false

description: Checks if all entries in a hashmap meets a specific condition.
examples:
import { HashMap } from "effect"

const map = HashMap.make(["a", 1], ["b", 2], ["c", 3])

console.log(HashMap.every(map, (value) => value > 0)) // true
console.log(HashMap.every(map, (value) => value > 1)) // false

description: A mutable hash map that stores key-value pairs and supports both referential and Effect structural equality. Operations mutate the map in place. Keys that implement `Equal` / `Hash` can be looked up structurally; other keys use normal JavaScript reference or primitive equality.
Checks if the specified value is a `MutableHashMap`, `false` otherwise.
Creates an empty MutableHashMap.
Creates a MutableHashMap from a variable number of key-value pairs.
examples:
import { MutableHashMap } from "effect"

const map = MutableHashMap.make(
  ["key1", 42],
  ["key2", 100],
  ["key3", 200]
)

console.log(MutableHashMap.get(map, "key1")) // Some(42)
console.log(MutableHashMap.size(map)) // 3

description: Creates a MutableHashMap from an iterable collection of key-value pairs.
Looks up a key in the `MutableHashMap`. Returns `Some(value)` when an equal key is present and `None` when the key is absent.
Returns an iterable over the keys in the `MutableHashMap`.
Returns an iterable over the values in the `MutableHashMap`.
Checks if the MutableHashMap contains the specified key.
Sets a key-value pair in the MutableHashMap, mutating the map in place. If the key already exists, its value is updated.
Updates the value of the specified key within the MutableHashMap if it exists. If the key doesn't exist, the map remains unchanged.

Sets or removes the specified key in the MutableHashMap using an update function. The function receives the current value as an Option and returns an Option. If the function returns Some, the key is set to that value. If the function returns None, the key is removed.
Removes the specified key from the MutableHashMap, mutating the map in place. If the key doesn't exist, the map remains unchanged.
Removes all key-value pairs from the MutableHashMap, mutating the map in place. The map becomes empty after this operation.
examples:
import { MutableHashMap } from "effect"

const map = MutableHashMap.make(
  ["key1", 42],
  ["key2", 100],
  ["key3", 200]
)

console.log(MutableHashMap.size(map)) // 3

// Clear all entries
MutableHashMap.clear(map)

console.log(MutableHashMap.size(map)) // 0
console.log(MutableHashMap.has(map, "key1")) // false

// Can still add new entries after clearing
MutableHashMap.set(map, "new", 999)
console.log(MutableHashMap.size(map)) // 1

description: Returns the number of key-value pairs in the MutableHashMap.
examples:
import { MutableHashMap } from "effect"

const map = MutableHashMap.empty<string, number>()
console.log(MutableHashMap.size(map)) // 0

MutableHashMap.set(map, "key1", 42)
MutableHashMap.set(map, "key2", 100)
console.log(MutableHashMap.size(map)) // 2

MutableHashMap.remove(map, "key1")
console.log(MutableHashMap.size(map)) // 1

MutableHashMap.clear(map)
console.log(MutableHashMap.size(map)) // 0

description: Returns `true` when the `MutableHashMap` contains no key-value pairs.
Runs a callback for each key-value pair in the `MutableHashMap`. Iteration follows the backing map's order. The callback receives the value first and the key second, matching `Map.prototype.forEach`.
Represents a readonly record with keys of type `K` and values of type `A`. This is the foundational type for immutable key-value mappings in Effect.

Represents a type that converts literal string keys to generic string type and symbol keys to generic symbol type. This is useful for maintaining type safety while allowing flexible key types in record operations.
Represents the intersection of two key types, handling both literal and non-literal string keys. This type is used in record operations that need to compute overlapping keys.
Type lambda for readonly records, used in higher-kinded type operations. This enables records to work with generic type constructors and functors.
examples:
import type { HKT, Record } from "effect"

type Settings = HKT.Kind<
  Record.ReadonlyRecordTypeLambda<"port" | "retries">,
  never,
  never,
  never,
  number
>

const defaults: Settings = {
  port: 3000,
  retries: 3
}

description: Creates a new, empty record.
Determine if a record is empty.
examples:
import { Record } from "effect"
import * as assert from "node:assert"

assert.deepStrictEqual(Record.isEmptyRecord({}), true)
assert.deepStrictEqual(Record.isEmptyRecord({ a: 3 }), false)

description: Determine if a record is empty.
examples:
import { Record } from "effect"
import * as assert from "node:assert"

assert.deepStrictEqual(Record.isEmptyReadonlyRecord({}), true)
assert.deepStrictEqual(Record.isEmptyReadonlyRecord({ a: 3 }), false)

description: Takes an iterable and a projection function and returns a record. The projection function maps each value of the iterable to a tuple of a key and a value, which is then added to the resulting record.
examples:
import { Record } from "effect"
import * as assert from "node:assert"

const input = [1, 2, 3, 4]

assert.deepStrictEqual(
  Record.fromIterableWith(input, (a) => [String(a), a * 2]),
  { "1": 2, "2": 4, "3": 6, "4": 8 }
)

description: Creates a new record from an iterable, utilizing the provided function to determine the key for each element.

Builds a record from an iterable of key-value pairs. If there are conflicting keys when using `fromEntries`, the last occurrence of the key/value pair will overwrite the previous ones. So the resulting record will only have the value of the last occurrence of each key.
examples:
import { Record } from "effect"
import * as assert from "node:assert"

const input: Array<[string, number]> = [["a", 1], ["b", 2]]

assert.deepStrictEqual(Record.fromEntries(input), { a: 1, b: 2 })

description: Transforms the values of a record into an `Array` with a custom mapping function.
examples:
import { Record } from "effect"
import * as assert from "node:assert"

const x = { a: 1, b: 2, c: 3 }
assert.deepStrictEqual(Record.collect(x, (key, n) => [key, n]), [["a", 1], [
  "b",
  2
], ["c", 3]])

description: Takes a record and returns an array of tuples containing its keys and values.
examples:
import { Record } from "effect"
import * as assert from "node:assert"

const x = { a: 1, b: 2, c: 3 }
assert.deepStrictEqual(Record.toEntries(x), [["a", 1], ["b", 2], ["c", 3]])

description: Returns the number of key/value pairs in a record.
examples:
import { Record } from "effect"
import * as assert from "node:assert"

assert.deepStrictEqual(Record.size({ a: "a", b: 1, c: true }), 3)

description: Check if a given `key` exists in a record.
examples:
import { Record } from "effect"
import * as assert from "node:assert"

assert.deepStrictEqual(Record.has({ a: 1, b: 2 }, "a"), true)
assert.deepStrictEqual(Record.has(Record.empty<string>(), "c"), false)

description: Retrieve a value at a particular key from a record, returning it wrapped in an `Option`.
examples:
import { Option, Record as R } from "effect"
import * as assert from "node:assert"

const person: Record<string, unknown> = { name: "John Doe", age: 35 }

assert.deepStrictEqual(R.get(person, "name"), Option.some("John Doe"))
assert.deepStrictEqual(R.get(person, "email"), Option.none())

description: Apply a function to the element at the specified key, creating a new record, or return `Option.none()` if the key doesn't exist.
examples:
import { Record } from "effect"

const f = (x: number) => x * 2

const input: Record<string, number> = { a: 3 }

Record.modify(input, "a", f) // Option.some({ a: 6 })
Record.modify(input, "b", f) // Option.none()

description: Replaces the value at an existing key and returns the updated record in `Option.some`. If the key is not present, returns `Option.none()` and leaves the record unchanged.
examples:
import { Record } from "effect"

Record.replace({ a: 1, b: 2, c: 3 }, "a", 10) // Option.some({ a: 10, b: 2, c: 3 })
Record.replace(Record.empty<string>(), "a", 10) // Option.none()

description: If the given key exists in the record, returns a new record with the key removed. If the key does not exist, returns a shallow copy of the original record.
examples:
import { Record } from "effect"
import * as assert from "node:assert"

assert.deepStrictEqual(Record.remove({ a: 1, b: 2 }, "a"), { b: 2 })

description: Retrieves the value of the property with the given `key` from a record and returns an `Option` of a tuple with the value and the record with the removed property. If the key is not present, returns `Option.none()`.
examples:
import { Record } from "effect"

const input: Record<string, number> = { a: 1, b: 2 }

Record.pop(input, "a") // Option.some([1, { b: 2 }])
Record.pop(input, "c") // Option.none()

description: Maps a record into another record by applying a transformation function to each of its values.
examples:
import { Record } from "effect"
import * as assert from "node:assert"

const f = (n: number) => `-${n}`

assert.deepStrictEqual(Record.map({ a: 3, b: 5 }, f), { a: "-3", b: "-5" })

const g = (n: number, key: string) => `${key.toUpperCase()}-${n}`

assert.deepStrictEqual(Record.map({ a: 3, b: 5 }, g), { a: "A-3", b: "B-5" })

description: Maps the keys of a `ReadonlyRecord` while preserving the corresponding values.
Maps entries of a `ReadonlyRecord` using the provided function, allowing modification of both keys and corresponding values.
Transforms a record by applying the function `f` to each key and value in the original record. If the function succeeds, the key-value pair is included in the output record.
examples:
import { Record, Result } from "effect"
import * as assert from "node:assert"

const x = { a: 1, b: 2, c: 3 }
const f = (a: number, key: string) => a > 2 ? Result.succeed(a * 2) : Result.failVoid
assert.deepStrictEqual(Record.filterMap(x, f), { c: 6 })

description: Selects properties from a record whose values match the given predicate.
examples:
import { Record } from "effect"
import * as assert from "node:assert"

const x = { a: 1, b: 2, c: 3, d: 4 }
assert.deepStrictEqual(Record.filter(x, (n) => n > 2), { c: 3, d: 4 })

description: Given a record with `Option` values, returns a new record containing only the `Some` values, preserving the original keys.
Given a record with `Result` values, returns a new record containing only the `Err` values, preserving the original keys.
Given a record with `Result` values, returns a new record containing only the `Ok` values, preserving the original keys.

Applies a function to each record entry and partitions the returned `Result` values into two records. Failure values are collected in the left record, and success values are collected in the right record, preserving the original keys.
Partitions a record of `Result` values into two separate records, one with the `Err` values and one with the `Ok` values.
examples:
import { Record, Result } from "effect"
import * as assert from "node:assert"

assert.deepStrictEqual(
  Record.separate({ a: Result.fail("e"), b: Result.succeed(1) }),
  [{ a: "e" }, { b: 1 }]
)

description: Retrieve the keys of a given record as an array.
examples:
import { Record } from "effect"
import * as assert from "node:assert"

assert.deepStrictEqual(Record.keys({ a: 1, b: 2, c: 3 }), ["a", "b", "c"])

description: Retrieve the values of a given record as an array.
examples:
import { Record } from "effect"
import * as assert from "node:assert"

assert.deepStrictEqual(Record.values({ a: 1, b: 2, c: 3 }), [1, 2, 3])

description: Add a new key-value pair or update an existing key's value in a record.
examples:
import { Record } from "effect"
import * as assert from "node:assert"

assert.deepStrictEqual(Record.set("a", 5)({ a: 1, b: 2 }), { a: 5, b: 2 })
assert.deepStrictEqual(Record.set("c", 5)({ a: 1, b: 2 }), { a: 1, b: 2, c: 5 })

description: Check if all the keys and values in one record are also found in another record. Uses the provided equivalence function to compare values.

Checks whether the first record is a subrecord of the second record. Returns `true` when every key and value in `self` is also present in `that`. Values are compared with Effect equality via `Equal.asEquivalence()`.
examples:
import { Record } from "effect"
import * as assert from "node:assert"

assert.deepStrictEqual(
  Record.isSubrecord({ a: 1 } as Record<string, number>, { a: 1, b: 2 }),
  true
)
assert.deepStrictEqual(
  Record.isSubrecord({ a: 1, b: 2 }, { a: 1 } as Record<string, number>),
  false
)

description: Reduce a record to a single value by combining its entries with a specified function.
examples:
import { Record } from "effect"
import * as assert from "node:assert"

assert.deepStrictEqual(
  Record.reduce({ a: 1, b: 2, c: 3 }, 0, (acc, value, key) => acc + value),
  6
)

description: Check if all entries in a record meet a specific condition.
examples:
import { Record } from "effect"
import * as assert from "node:assert"

assert.deepStrictEqual(Record.every({ a: 1, b: 2 }, (n) => n > 0), true)
assert.deepStrictEqual(Record.every({ a: 1, b: -1 }, (n) => n > 0), false)

description: Check if any entry in a record meets a specific condition.
examples:
import { Record } from "effect"
import * as assert from "node:assert"

assert.deepStrictEqual(Record.some({ a: 1, b: 2 }, (n) => n > 1), true)
assert.deepStrictEqual(Record.some({ a: 1, b: 2 }, (n) => n > 2), false)

description: Merge two records, preserving entries that exist in either of the records. For keys that exist in both records, the provided combine function is used to merge the values.
Merge two records, retaining only the entries that exist in both records. For intersecting keys, the provided combine function is used to merge the values.
Merge two records, preserving only the entries that are unique to each record. Keys that exist in both records are excluded from the result.
Create an `Equivalence` for records using the provided `Equivalence` for values. Two records are considered equivalent if they have the same keys and their corresponding values are equivalent.
examples:
import { Equal, Record } from "effect"
import * as assert from "node:assert"

const recordEquivalence = Record.makeEquivalence(Equal.asEquivalence<number>())

assert.deepStrictEqual(recordEquivalence({ a: 1, b: 2 }, { a: 1, b: 2 }), true)
assert.deepStrictEqual(recordEquivalence({ a: 1, b: 2 }, { a: 1, b: 3 }), false)

description: Create a non-empty record from a single element.
examples:
import { Record } from "effect"
import * as assert from "node:assert"

assert.deepStrictEqual(Record.singleton("a", 1), { a: 1 })

description: A `Reducer` for combining `Record`s using union. Values for keys that exist in both records are combined using the provided `Combiner`.

Creates a `Reducer` whose `combine` operation intersects two records and combines values for keys present in both records. The reducer's `initialValue` is an empty record. Because intersection with an empty record is empty, the default `combineAll` folds from `{}` and therefore produces `{}` for ordinary non-empty inputs.
Returns the first entry that satisfies the specified predicate, or `None` if no such entry exists.
